verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
The patient was started on amiodarone during his cardiac surgery on 11/21/2023, as per the patient chart for Cards_Patient 14. During his follow up visit at cardiac rehab on 1/23/2024, amiodarone had been discontinued and was no longer listed on his med list.

"Hospital Course:  Mr. Michelsen presented to Triad Eye Institute PLLC and was brought to the operating room on 11/21/23. He underwent a CABG x 4 utilizing LIMA to LAD, SVG sequentially to Diagonal 1 and OM, and SVG to distal RCA. He tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to the SICU in stable condition.  The patient was extubated the evening of surgery.  The patient's pacing wires were removed without difficulty.  He was transitioned off IV Amiodarone to an oral regimen.  He has chronic atrial fibrillation. "  Clinical Notes - Hospital Course - Column D - Row 99

verifier_values.criteria: States that amiodarone can cause hepatis steatosis.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
"Medications that have been linked to liver steatosis include amiodarone, glucocorticoids, methotrexate, and tamoxifen [48-50]."

https://www.uptodate.com/contents/clinical-features-and-diagnosis-of-metabolic-dysfunction-associated-steatotic-liver-disease-nonalcoholic-fatty-liver-disease-in-adults

verifier_values.criteria: States that the patient's CT scan showed no evidence of hepatic steatosis 6 months after amiodarone was discontinued.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
"08/05/2024 CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast:
The liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenals and kidneys have a normal appearance."   Patient 14 Radiology

Amiodarone had been discontinued in January 2024.  A CT scan was done approximately 6 months after amiodarone was discontinued. The CT scan report from 8/5/2024 shows no abnormal liver findings.

verifier_values.criteria: States the amiodarone is unlikely to be the cause of the patient’s hepatic steatosis.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
"A transient rise in serum aminotransferase concentrations (aspartate aminotransferase [AST], alanine aminotransferase [ALT]) occurs in approximately 25 percent (range 15 to 50 percent) of patients soon after amiodarone is begun [46]. "  https://www.uptodate.com/contents/amiodarone-clinical-uses-administration-and-adverse-effects

The patient's CT scan 6 months after amiodarone had been discontinued did not show any signs of hepatic steatosis.  This makes it highly unlikely that amiodarone is the cause of the patient's hepatic steatosis.

verifier_values.criteria: States that the patient’s liver biopsy shows hepatic steatosis with fibrosis.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation: "Diagnosis: Metabolic dysfunction-associated liver disease."  "Gross Description: Liver parenchyma shows nodules with steatosis"  "Microscopic Description: Extensive macrovesicular steatosis with prominent balloon degeneration. Perisinusoidal fibrosis with portal fibrosis."   Patient 14 Report

verifier_values.criteria: States that metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) with stage 2 fibrosis is the most likely primary diagnosis.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
"Establishing the diagnosis — The diagnosis of MASLD is typically established on the basis of imaging demonstrating liver steatosis in the absence of other primary etiologies (eg, drug-induced liver injury) in patients with at least one metabolic risk factor (figure 1) [4]. We typically reserve diagnostic liver biopsy for patients with suspected MASLD when the diagnosis remains uncertain despite laboratory and imaging tests."   

"MASLD with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) – Patients with MASH have histologic evidence of inflammation and hepatocellular injury, such as ballooning of hepatocytes, with or without fibrosis. This category was formerly known as nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH)."

"Stage: Fibrosis stage 2 (F2)	  Description:  Zone 3 perisinusoidal fibrosis with portal fibrosis"

https://www.uptodate.com/contents/clinical-features-and-diagnosis-of-metabolic-dysfunction-associated-steatotic-liver-disease-nonalcoholic-fatty-liver-disease-in-adults

verifier_values.criteria: States that GLP-1 (Glucagon like peptide) based therapy is a recommended treatment for overweight patients with diabetes.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation: "If a strong indication for body weight management is present, particularly in the setting of obesity-related comorbidities (eg, obstructive sleep apnea, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis), GLP-1-based therapy is an effective option for initial treatment."  https://www.uptodate.com/contents/initial-management-of-hyperglycemia-in-adults-with-type-2-diabetes-mellitus

verifier_values.criteria: States that semaglutide has been approved to treat MASH with liver fibrosis.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation: "The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has approved semaglutide (Wegovy) injection for the treatment of adults with metabolic dysfunction–associated steatohepatitis (MASH) and moderate to advanced liver fibrosis, making it the first glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist (GLP-1 RA) available for this indication."  https://www.docwirenews.com/post/fda-grants-accelerated-approval-of-semaglutide-for-treatment-of-mash-with-liver-fibrosis

verifier_values.criteria: States that semaglutide is the recommended first-line treatment for this patient.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
The patient has MASH associated with type 2 diabetes and overweight, which is a strong indication for GLP-1 therapy, most notably semaglutide which has been approved by the FDA in 2025 for this indication.

"GLP-1-based therapy – For patients with MASH and stage ≥2 fibrosis, we suggest GLP-1-based therapy for treatment of MASH (Grade 2C). In addition to weight loss, GLP-1-based therapy improves steatohepatitis and fibrosis. Most patients with MASH also have another indication for GLP-1-based therapy (diabetes, obesity, overweight with weight-related comorbidity)... We suggest resmetirom, a thyroid hormone receptor-beta agonist, for patients with MASH and fibrosis stage F2 or F3 (table 2) who do not achieve sustained weight loss [36-38]. This includes patients who cannot access (or cannot tolerate) GLP-1-based therapy, as well as those for whom GLP-1-based therapy does not result in sufficient weight loss to normalize transaminases. "    
https://www.uptodate.com/contents/management-of-metabolic-dysfunction-associated-steatotic-liver-disease-nonalcoholic-fatty-liver-disease-in-adults

The uptodate guidelines are based on recent guidance from the FDA on semaglutide:
"The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has approved Wegovy (semaglutide) injection to treat metabolic-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) in adults with moderate-to-advanced fibrosis (excessive scar tissue in the liver). MASH, also known as nonalcoholic steatohepatitis, is a serious liver disease. Wegovy, which was first approved in 2017, is also approved for obesity or overweight and to reduce cardiovascular events, such as heart attacks, in individuals at high risk of these events. Approximately 6% of U.S. adults (14.9 million people) have MASH, and its prevalence is expanding."  
https://www.fda.gov/drugs/news-events-human-drugs/fda-approves-treatment-serious-liver-disease-known-mash
